formLanguage: broad axial palace composition with large ceremonial negative spaces
layered Korean timber halls with dark hip-and-gable tiled roofs
low urban belt and dense green tree belt behind the palace
asymmetric mountain skyline with a lower rocky left ridge and a taller right peak
aerial perspective with warm foreground courts and cool hazed distance
structureKind: deep action-ready hierarchy of palace systems and repeated architectural bays
staged foreground, palace, vegetation, city, and mountain depth layers
deterministic instanced vegetation and urban massing
custom deformed roof and ridge terrain geometry
motionPotential: hinged outer and inner gate leaves
topology-preserving Layer Lens visibility and emphasis states
materialFamilies: charcoal ceramic roof tile with layered ridge and eave response
painted timber and restrained dancheong accents
pale stone and plaster podiums and walls
warm sandy courtyard with compacted path variation
cool receding city masonry and glazing
objectClass.notes: The target is the complete photographed palace campus and its city-tree-mountain context, not an exact survey model of a named building. The 842x476 source supports an approximate stylized real-time reconstruction. Existing preview geometry is provenance only and must not be promoted into the production base.

reasoning: The source is a crowded architectural landscape rather than an isolated object, so identity depends on the relationship between many systems rather than one silhouette.

The foreground outer gate, broad processional courts, inner gate, main hall, secondary halls, walls, corridors, tree belt, city, and mountains are all separately readable and require a deep hierarchy.
Korean roof identity depends on curved lifted eaves, layered fascia, pitch, overhang, ridge caps, end ornaments, rafters, and tile rhythm; plain prisms cannot satisfy the reference.
The mountain skyline is composition-critical and asymmetric. It requires custom seeded ridge geometry, layered forest and rock response, and atmospheric recession rather than cone primitives.

The low-resolution single aerial view hides rear faces, exact bay counts, and fine dancheong patterns. Those details must be inferred conservatively and documented instead of claimed as recovered geometry.
Production variants, camera sockets, gates, Layer Lens groups, colliders, and protected architecture require stable action-ready nodes even though most architecture remains static.

specDepthDecision.rationale: A shallow palace-block plus cone-mountain scene fails the reference. Production readiness requires six staged macro systems, separate halls/gates/courts/corridors, reusable roof topology, deterministic city/vegetation/terrain repetition, independent material layers, and multi-view visual evidence.

resolvedInferenceLimits: Rear and hidden side elevations are not visible; infer them bilaterally from the visible axial and side-wing logic.
Exact historic building identity, dimensions, bay counts, and roof ornament profiles are below source resolution; use reference-consistent proportions rather than exact reconstruction claims.
The source contains baked midday lighting, haze, and camera response; material channels cannot be uniquely recovered and must pass confidence-gated extraction plus neutral relighting.
Distant city block identity is unreadable; preserve bounded massing, road gaps, and atmospheric value structure rather than individual buildings.

definitionOfDone: At the reference aerial camera, the scene immediately reads as the photographed Seoul palace campus rather than a generic East Asian compound.
The outer gate, inner gate, and main throne hall form a clear receding axis across broad warm courtyards with controlled entrances and side walls.
Every primary hall uses a reusable Korean hip-and-gable roof system with curved lifted eaves, layered fascia, ridge and end caps, tile or rafter rhythm, and credible podium-column-roof contact.
The palace remains the identity focus while the city, tree belt, and mountain layers create the source depth ordering without random clutter.
The mountain silhouette preserves the lower rocky left ridge, central saddle, and taller steep right peak under reference framing.
The asset exposes deterministic seeds, stable layer groups, named cameras and sockets, gate pivots, collider metadata, protected destruction groups, and production-safe variant controls.
The final base and every curated production variant have fresh full-image comparison evidence with an overall AI-vision score of at least 0.86 and every selected critical feature at least 0.86.

featureGroups.name: Outer gate to inner gate to main hall axial hierarchy
qualityCriteria: The outer gate anchors the lower-left foreground, the inner gate crosses the central processional axis, and the main hall remains the largest palace focal mass.
Each gate and hall has a stable pivot, podium contact, controlled opening, and parent link inside the palace-axis system.
The hierarchy remains unchanged across all production variants.
failureModes: the gates and main hall read as equal toy blocks
the processional axis bends or loses the ordered gate count
secondary halls compete with or obscure the main hall

featureGroups.name: Korean hip-and-gable tiled roof system
qualityCriteria: Primary roofs show pitched hip-and-gable planes, center sag, lifted corners, layered eaves, fascia, ridge caps, hip ridges, and end ornaments.
Tile rows and rafters follow the deformed roof surface with deterministic spacing and safe instancing.
Columns and beams visibly support roof loads through podium contact; no roof floats above a generic box.
failureModes: plain triangular prism or flat slab roofs
uniform eaves without lifted corners or layered fascia
tile, ridge, or ornament repetition detaches from the roof surface

featureGroups.name: Courtyard, wall, and ceremonial negative-space proportions
qualityCriteria: The foreground and main processional courts remain broad enough to dominate the palace ground plane.
Perimeter walls and corridor wings frame rather than fill the courts, with entrances aligned to the palace axis.
Tree and city distributions preserve the large source clearings.
failureModes: courtyards shrink into narrow paths between oversized halls
walls or vegetation close the ceremonial sightline
random detail removes the reference negative spaces

featureGroups.name: City to tree belt to mountain atmospheric depth
qualityCriteria: Low bounded city massing sits behind the palace with explicit road and green gaps.
A darker tree belt separates urban blocks from mountain foothills.
Successive mountain layers reduce contrast, saturation, and detail with haze while keeping the palace foreground readable.
failureModes: city blocks become random high-rise clutter
tree belt and mountains collapse into one green wall
haze crushes palace roof contrast or erases the skyline

featureGroups.name: Asymmetric mountain silhouette and aerial composition
qualityCriteria: The left skyline stays lower, broad, and rock-patched; the central saddle remains open; the right peak is steeper and highest.
Mountain terrain is custom seeded ridge geometry rather than cones or symmetric noise mounds.
The reference camera preserves the palace-to-sky balance and the mountain peaks remain inside the 1.769 frame.
failureModes: cone mountains or a mirrored skyline
the right peak is lower than the left ridge
camera framing crops the skyline or gives mountains more visual weight than the palace

visualDeltaChecks: outer-gate, inner-gate, and main-hall centerline and projected-size delta
courtyard width-to-depth, wall-height, and occupied-to-empty ground-plane delta
roof pitch, overhang, eave lift, ridge length, and tile/rafter rhythm delta
city/tree/mountain depth ordering, value range, and haze delta
left-ridge, central-saddle, and right-peak skyline delta
roof, timber, stone, courtyard, vegetation, mountain, and city albedo/roughness/normal/AO response delta
reference camera field of view, elevation, target, horizon, and palace-to-sky balance delta
Layer Lens isolation delta without topology, parent-link, socket, or collider mutation
antiShallowSpecRules: Do not proceed to code if qualityContract.qualityBar is unassessed.
Do not proceed to code if the spec only contains a root component for a moderate or complex object.
Do not proceed to code if required featureGroups are not represented by componentTree, materials, or repetitionSystems.
Do not proceed to code if visible local features are described only in prose and not attached to components/materials/evidenceRefs.
Do not proceed past structural-pass if attached child parts lack attachment.parentSocket, localStart, localEnd, embedDepth/overlap, and gapTolerance.
Do not pass material look-dev when albedo is reused as roughness, height, normal, or AO.
Do not pass material look-dev without macro, meso, and micro surface frequency bands for close-up materials.
Do not pass reference-fidelity material look-dev from a source image without usable referencePbr maps or an explicit documented limitation.
Do not patch a spec with extracted PBR maps when extraction confidence is below the target threshold unless the user explicitly accepts lower fidelity.
Do not reuse, upscale, or cosmetically polish the preview cone-mountain or toy-block implementation as the production base.
Do not use cone primitives for mountains or triangular prisms as the final Korean roof system.

Do not continue a visual pass without a browser-rendered screenshot, one full reference/render comparison, AI-vision layer and semantic-feature scores, concrete mismatch notes, and an evidence-bound review entry.
Do not let a global score hide a selected critical feature below 0.86.

Do not change the palace axis, gate count or order, main-hall identity, roof topology, parent links, sockets, action pivots, reference camera, collider topology, or protected review targets in production variants.
Do not generate production variants until the production base is meaningful and accepted through the locked pass gates.
Do not claim exact palace dimensions, exact hidden elevations, or physically recovered PBR from this single low-resolution image.

authoringInstruction: Assessment completed from direct visual inspection and technical probing. Generate the production ObjectSculptSpec from this contract, then replace all generic starter review targets before strict validation.
